sentences.text: Well, on the 8th, as they assembled us and they told us they're going to blow us up, we then went back to our barracks.
No one slept.
We were wondering what time the barrack will go up in flames or whatever happens when you blow something up.
No one slept.
No one talked.
We came so close.
And now that would be the end.
It wasn't so difficult for us to be ready to die while the war was on.
But now, having lived through all that horror, now, now we want to live.
In the morning, we woke up.
It was quiet all night.
We look out the window, and there's no one in the barracks where the SS used to be.
No one was yelling and screaming to get up, to go to work.
It was quiet, an eerie silence.
We were afraid to walk out of the barracks.
We thought any minute that the barracks go off, the dynamite goes off.
And so we sat there.
All of a sudden, we heard steps, footsteps of soldiers.
Now we didn't know what to make out of it.
And there were our friends, the prisoners of war, marched to the camp, cut through the barbed wire, walked in, and said, girls, you are free.
This was the moment we were waiting for so many years, hoping and praying for.
And we had such beautiful illusions what will happen, the Americans will take us to Bremerhof and put us on ships, and we'll go to this wonderful country called America, and we'll start a new life.
We never even thought that we are alone.
Now when they said to us, you're free, it all suddenly-- the realization of where we are and who we are came.
Everybody walked around.
No one spoke.
There was silence in camp and everybody deep in their thoughts.
Where are our parents?
Are they alive, the brothers, the cousins, the family?
We are free.
Free to do what, to go where, and with whom?
No education, no parents, no family, no home, no money, no country that wants us.
What do we do?
Children that grew old in camp in years but never made a decision-- we never decided where we want to work, what time we want to go up, what we want to eat, what we're going to wear.
And now we are free.
We'll have to learn to trust humanity again, to begin a new life, but where, and how?
Was there no liberating army that came to the camp?
The Soviets rolled in with their tanks in the evening.
The reason why they were held up till 9th of May is because they couldn't decide who's going to take this town.
All of Europe was liberated.
We were the last ones.
So the Soviets rolled in.
And we heard the noise.
We didn't know what it was, the tanks rolling on the pavement.
We ran through the factory ground and pulled off all the tulips and all the spring flowers that grew in the garden.
And we're throwing it at the soldiers.
And all of a sudden, there was one in an armored car with a red hat and probably a very high officer, had all kind of stars.
And he jumped off that armored car, and he said, [NON-ENGLISH]?
Are you Jewish?
We said, yes.
And he started speaking Yiddish and told us, where is your camp?
And so we said, come with us.
And we came to the camp with his armored car.
And he stood on that car, and he says, I have-- you are free now.
But I beg you, don't tell my soldiers where the camp is.
These are men that were now in the war for so long.
They will rape any woman they see.
Oh, well, but we are Jewish.
We are the victims.
We're not Germans.
He says, I can't guarantee you safety.
And so we all retreated back in the barracks, closed the doors, moved the bunk beds to the doors and to the windows, and sat there.
The same night, somebody tried to get in and kicked in the door, couldn't move it.
And 45 women in one room started to scream.
And he started to run away.
The reason that we screamed, we didn't know if the guy is inside or out.
And there were two, three people in one bed.
And if she happened to have-- a friend happened to have your hand on you or near you, you didn't know who it was in that moment.
You thought it would be full of Russians in our room.
Well, we screamed so much, that they run away.
But we came up with an idea.
We wrote-- found a piece of paper and some pencils in the SS guards' offices.
And we wrote 'Typhoid Fever' and put it on every door.
And the next night when they came, they ran away.
And that saved us.
This is why I say women's experiences are different than those of men.
Here were the liberators, when they liberated as a camp Buchenwald and Bergen-Belsen.
The Americans and the British rolled in with the truck with the food and with the medication and with the doctors.
We had to hide, afraid to be raped.
That was our liberation.
We were afraid to go back home.
We stayed in the camp for days and then in groups.
We didn't know anyone is alive.
We thought we were the only ones that survived, till one young man came from a neighboring town, a Jewish man, and says, I come from a concentration camp.
And so many men survived in our camp.
And so he told us, go home, but go in groups.
You'll be safe.
We then asked the owner of the factory, who was the biggest Nazi you can imagine, but he was so scared of us, that we asked him to give us a piece of paper that we worked in the factory.
And I do have that piece of paper.
And this was what we had with us.
It was our identification.
And in groups, we got whatever moved, say it was a train.
But the bridges were blown up.
You'd come to the bridge, that was it.
We'd get on a boat.
Whatever moved, we got on it just to get home.
We arrived in a Czech town called Pardubice.
Well, my sister wasn't feeling well.
And so we went to the Red Cross station.
And they realized that she had typhoid fever, and they arrested her.
The police came, arrested her-- they didn't want to spread the illness-- and took her to the hospital.
We had nowhere to stay.
And my mother said, well she's in the hospital taken care of.
And we were gone.
By the way, we were the only three people in the room of 45 that didn't have typhoid fever during the war.
There was someone that was really, really taking care of us.
And if children ask me if I still believe in God, I have all kind of answers.
Every episode where my life was bad is another reason why I still believe in God.
So my sister stayed behind in a hospital.
My mother and I went back home.
Because when my father left, he says, this is where we meet.
If we have to crawl, wherever we are, if we are separated, no matter how long it takes us, this is where we meet.
And so that's where we went.
And we came by train, the last part of our journey.
How long did it take you to get there?
I don't remember.
Time had no-- in the process, we were on a platform on a train, and I had a bundle of letters from my father and a pair of shoes in which I came to camp.
And I didn't wear those shoes.
I didn't dare to wear them because I wanted a pair of shoes when I be liberated.
How strong was my belief in being liberated and survive, that I wouldn't wear the shoes.
I put straw around my feet.
And here I had those shoes wrapped on the laces and the bundle with my father's letters.
And I fell asleep on that platform.
And a Russian soldier cut off the string, and he threw away my precious, precious letters from my father that he wrote from camp and my shoes, the only pair I had.
We lost-- now I lost everything.
But we still were hoping that my father will meet us at home.
Right.
And we arrived in Bielszowice in the station.
And I said, everybody knew us.
And the station master says, my God, you're alive!
And I said, did my father come back?
And he says, I didn't see him.
At that point my mother said, he will not come back.
He'd have been-- he would have been the first one.
We went back to our home.
The people lived in our house.
And the woman said, I'm going to my parents.
This is your place.
Her furniture was there, but she says, this is your home now.
I'll go and stay with my mother, with my husband.
So that shows you that there were nice people in the town and what they thought of us.
And so we stayed there, waited till my sister came back a few-- two weeks later.
So she got better.
They released her, and she came.
My father never returned.
And my mother just didn't get out of bed.
She just didn't want to live.
So I called a friend of my father's who was a doctor in the town.
He went to school with him.
And he called me out, and he says, leave this place.
She will not live.
She doesn't want to live.
She can't live here.
And so my sister found a place in lower Silesia called Reichenbach.
And there were a lot of survivors, young men, young women.
And she says, you know, they have places where they threw out the Germans of their homes, and there are young people there.
Let's go there.
So we packed.
We didn't have much, but whatever we had.
We left.
And we went to this town, And we got a place to live.
And I worked for the Russian army.
I was sewing uniforms.
At any point did you think of going to a DP camp?
We didn't know about them.
What we did plan is to go to Palestine.
And we had planned to go, because we couldn't go legally.
I was by then 19 years old.
And we couldn't go legally.
So we decided, a bunch of us, some young men and-- my mother went reluctantly, but she followed us, kind of got with-- caught in that storm.
We'll go through Bratislava-- through Czechoslovakia, down to Hungary, Turkey, wherever the road take us.
We can't go through the Mediterranean.
How we going to get on the boat?
That's how we're going to go.
We crossed seven borders illegally.
And that's an Odyssey in itself.
I was caught.
I jumped off the train.
Then I jumped on a train again.
My mother and my sister and the group continued.
They got off the train.
We met up again.
Seven borders we crossed.
And we arrived in Bratislava.
We arrived in Bratislava ere of Rosh Hashanah.
And we were looking for a Jewish committee or organization that would tell us where to go, what to do, where to sleep.
So we found out from the Czechs that there is a school, a Jewish school.
You see, they were liberated so much earlier.
There was-- life was normal already.
So we went to the school building, and we sat down on the stairs of the school.
But it was locked.
It was closed.
It was ere of Rosh Hashanah.
And all of a sudden, we see the women running with the challahs and with the cake to the bakeries.
Because in Europe, you make the challah, and you go to the bakery to bake it.
They don't have big ovens at home.
And they run with the challahs, and they run back from the bakery with the challahs.
And then a man run to the mikvah, and they run back from the mikvah.
It's almost ere of yontif, and no one stops to ask, who are you?
Why are you sitting here?
Are you Jewish?
What are you-- it's yontif.
Where are you going?
Nobody.
Nobody bothered to ask who we are.
The three of you were together?
Right.
Right, and a few other people with us.
And we sat there.
So we decided to go to the Red Cross.
And they put us up in the school.
And wouldn't you know, middle of the night, Russian soldiers came into the classroom.
My mother, my sister, and I, we threw a bench at them.
It's this story of my life.
And we realized there is no point.
If we can't even be safe as women in Bratislava in a cultured country, how are we going to get through Turkey or Lebanon to get to Palestine?
This is a hopeless mission.
And my mother, and my sister, and I decided to turn back.
And go where, back to Bielszowice?
No.
We heard that the Allied have set up camps for Holocaust survivors.
But my mother still was not ready to go to a camp again.
But she knew all these resorts, Austria and around there.
Because the family was always going to these resorts, in Czechoslovakia, to Carlsbad, Marienbad, Bad Gastein in Austria, and so on.
And so she was hoping-- she says, you know, we're going to go to these places.
We'll get-- we'll settle there and see what we're going to do.
My sister was ahead.
I said I knew how to sew.
We spoke the language.
We'll start a new life, see what we can do.
We stopped in a town.
We went from door to door, from hotel to hotel.
Either they didn't have anything or they realized we are Jews and didn't want to let us have anything.
We didn't exactly look like them.
So we went to the police station.
We said, we are Jewish.
We are survivors.
We were hoping for some sympathy.
So he says, the only place I can put you, in the jail.
You have some beds in the jail.
You want to sleep in a jail?
Well, thank you very much.
I was three years locked up.
I have to go behind bar-- behind bars again?
We went back to the station.
We got-- jumped into a coal car.
And wherever the train went, all night, back and forth, back and forth, we arrived in Munich.
We jumped out of the train.
We all looked at each other.
We looked like chimney sweeps.
We went up.
We jumped, climbed up on the locomotive when the locomotive was taking water, because they were the steam engines.
We were washing up in that water that was going into the locomotive.
We couldn't show ourselves anywhere looking the way we did.
As we walk out of the train station, we had a password, survivors, when they met.
And it was not hard to recognize them.
You say, [NON-ENGLISH]?
You my people?
And so that's what we did.
People that you kind of thought were survivors.
[NON-ENGLISH]?
Yes.
What we do?
Where do we go?
Oh, go to the German museum in Munich.
There they will register you as a survivor.
And they will send you to a DP camp.
One of them said, tell them you have a cousin in Landsberg.
This is the best camp.
So that's what we did.
We lied.
We said we had a cousin in Landsberg, and we just found out he's alive.
And we want to-- we can't wait to meet.
We have nobody else.
It was now ere of Yom Kippur when we arrived in that camp in Landsberg.
1945, ere of Yom Kippur.
Three women arrive.
We go to the-- they also had like a police in that camp, but it was a different-- now we're talking about a different situation.
So we went to the so-called police, or committee, whatever you want to call it.
And there was a very nice gentleman who was in charge.
And we said that we are alone and we don't know where to go.
And he called up one of the so-called policemen.
He says, hey, you, your sister lives in one room, you live in another.
You better move in with your sister.
These people need a roof over their heads.
And he says, but I need the room.
He says, just for a few days till we find something for them.
Well, PS-- we never moved out of that room.
And he found something else.
And we had a roof over our head.
Now we were in a DP camp.
No.
In the DP camp, that was American zone.
Now we didn't have to worry about the Russians raping us.
The food was coming from the United Nations, from UNRRA.
Clothing was being sent from the States.
Unfortunately, the Americans didn't realize who they sending clothes for.
There were shoes size triple A.
What they couldn't sell in the stores, they send for the refugees.
We got nothing but tuna.
I never heard of tuna, but I guess it was the cheapest.
But we're glad that we had food now.
There was a kitchen set up, and we would go.
We could eat there, or we could take the soup in our room.
And basically, you did not have to work.
You were provided for.
But you were waiting for an opportunity to go to a country, to where you can start a new life.
There were reunions.
People found each other.
Husbands and wives found each other.
There were many tragedies, where husband didn't know the wife was alive and remarried.
And suddenly, he had two wives.
Well, it was interesting.
There was a committee set up in Munich.
I do not recall-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] even if you go back to the camp when we were liberated, there wasn't a doctor that would come and visit the camp.
Can we help you?
Can we do something for you?
There wasn't a priest, a local priest.
I don't care what religion you are.
If you believe in God, you're there to comfort people in need.
We were on our own.
No one brought us food.
No one brought us medication.
No one took the sick and took them into a hospital.
We were on our own.
And here we were in DP camp.
They set up a hospital in that camp.
The young people found mates, got married.
There was no one to stand with them under the chuppah, and there was no mother to take them and father to give them away.
When we found survivors in Reichenbach.
Right.

Because mostly the young men that survived have come to Auschwitz, some through Bergen-- no, Bergen-Belsen didn't come that far down-- but Buchenwald, the other camps, Flossenburg, all these other-- Gleiwitz-- all the other camps, Pustkow, and so on.
So that's where we heard of the mass murder.
While we were in camp, we didn't really know what was happening.
We found out after the war.
We were isolated.
The only time that we found out that people were deported was when people that were deported, let's say to Auschwitz, were separated from their parents, and taken to concentration camp.
But they also didn't know what happened to their parents.
They just knew that they were separated from them.
Some knew, because I hear that when they arrived in Auschwitz, they were told, you see the chimney?
That's where you going out in a few hours, through the chimney.
So some knew.
But it wasn't really well known.
It took years to find out all that was happening.
How long did you remain in Landsberg?
In Landsberg I stayed four years.
Still did not give up hope that my father will not find us.
We put-- as we were going from town to town, and we only could get food from Red Cross, as we were going home and coming back, wherever was a Red Cross station, we would go and ask for food.
And that's what we would get.
And as we were there, we would sign our name on the wall.
And as a survivor signed their names, and before long the walls were filled with names.
And we were always looking-- maybe my father passed by, and maybe his name is on the wall.
And maybe someone from my family, anybody-- anybody-- a cousin, an uncle, an aunt, or anyone. 172 people my mother counted in my family, in extended family.
Nobody survived.
My sister, my mother, myself.
Well, we-- I was there four years.
I could not go anywhere, because I did not have relatives in the States.
My family was well-to-do, and they didn't have need to leave.
And when they wanted to leave, it was too late.

My mother went as far as writing to President Truman and telling him that because my father had not survived and he was a German citizen being born in Breslau, we are now penalized because we are now categorized as Polish citizens.
Because when I was born, my sister was born, we were Polish.
But if my father would have survived, we would have been Germans, and we would have been put on a German quota.
Well, we never heard from Truman.
But she tried.
She always tried.
We still thought maybe my father survived.
We thought maybe he had typhoid fever.
Maybe he had amnesia.
Maybe somebody hit him over the head and he doesn't know who he was. 50 Years I was looking for my father.
Never give up when you don't bury your loved one.
You can't believe he's dead.
Even last November in 1995, I was going to the Elie Wiesel dinner in New York.
And I was honored with other survivors.
And there was a name and a picture of a man, and underneath it says, Camp Oberlangen, [?
Bilau. ?] I immediately called the Israel bond office, got his name, and called him.
And I got together with him in New York, hoping that he'll tell me what happened to my father.
I'm still looking for people who'll tell me if he's really dead.
I will always be searching.
In 1949, my sister was already in Israel because she went with the exodus to then-Palestine.

And when the British returned them to Germany and they were behind barbed wire in a camp called Emden in North Germany after the British brought all the exodus people back, they were the first ones when Israel became a state to be taken out and taken to Israel.
When my sister was in Israel and mother and I had nowhere to go, no country wanted us, we decided to go to Israel.
And we left in-- before Passover, 1949, via Marseilles.
I met my husband there.
He was working, trying to get people on the boats.
And we-- he decided when he met me to go on the same boat to Israel.
His name is Lior, or Lazer was his name, Frieberg.
His father and his two brothers that survived were already in Israel.
He lost one brother.
But two brothers survived.
So they were already in Israel.
So he decided to go to Israel, too.
And so we had arrived in my sister's area of [NON-ENGLISH].
And we lived in Jaffa.
That's where she got her apartment.
Because as a refugee, she got an apartment.
Her husband was in the army.
And she was working as a hairdresser.
And we lived with her.
She had two rooms.
So we took one.
And then I got engaged.
We looked for a place in Tel Aviv, and we found a room.
And I would never, never leave my mother.
Everywhere I went, my mother always was with me.
I still was taking care of her every step of the way.
I provided for her during and after the war.
If I had a piece of bread, she was getting her part of it.
Never ate--
I-- I'm a designer, self-taught, clothes, couturiere, what we call in French.
And sewing saved my life in camp as well.
Because I was-- would repair things for the girls when they needed something changed or sewn or made from two blouses, one blouse, and for a piece of bread.
And then I shared it with my sister and with my mother.
And we always had a little bit of bread, a little more bread than somebody else, because of my sewing.
And in Israel, I was working as a-- in a-- in a couturiere's very elegant place.
And my husband was a carpenter, worked as a carpenter.
I became pregnant.
I was so worried about the Arabs wanting to throw us into the ocean.
I felt I paid my dues.
I wanted to live in a country in freedom.
